steps.title: More maths than plus and times

narration.default: Python's basic maths covers plus, minus, times, divide. But what about square roots, powers, the value of pi, rounding decimals? Python has a math module — a bundle of built-in maths tools you unlock with one line: import math. Same kind of import you used for random.

Type the very first line:

import math

import is standard Python — it loads a bundle of tools so the rest of your code can use them. After this line, anything inside math is available — written as math.toolname.

Type two lines:

print("Pi:", math.pi)
print("Square root of 16:", math.sqrt(16))

math.pi is the famous circle constant, about 3.14159. math.sqrt finds the square root — for 16 it gives 4.0 (the dot-zero means it's a decimal, not a whole number). Both standard Python.

Type three more lines:

print("2 to the power 10:", math.pow(2, 10))
print("Round up 3.2:", math.ceil(3.2))
print("Round down 3.8:", math.floor(3.8))

math.pow takes a base and an exponent — two to the power ten gives 1024. ceil rounds UP to the next whole number, so 3.2 becomes 4. floor rounds DOWN, so 3.8 becomes 3. Useful when you need a guaranteed whole answer.

Click Run. The terminal shows the value of pi, the square root of sixteen, two to the power ten, three-point-two rounded up, and three-point-eight rounded down. Five lines of maths, none of which Python's plus-minus-times-divide could do on their own.

math is one of dozens of modules that come built into every Python install — random was another. Anywhere your code needs more than basic maths, math has the tool. Want the area of a circle? math.pi times radius times radius. The diagonal of a screen? math.sqrt of width-squared plus height-squared. Round a price up to the next rupee? math.ceil. Try math.factorial of 5, or math.log of 100 — each one is a single line, and the heavy maths is done for you.
